_disclaimer: ALL ITEMS IN THIS FILE ARE SYNTHETIC PLACEHOLDERS, hand-authored to exercise the LSAT Item contract. They are NOT real LSAT items and must NOT be used as study material. Real items must be sourced from official LSAC PrepTests with redistribution licensing (D-SR11). Every item carries SyntheticFlag=SYNTHETIC.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new transit line will reduce commute times for workers traveling downtown. Therefore, workers will arrive at work less stressed and will be more productive.
items.Stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
items.ChoiceA: the transit line will be used by a majority of downtown workers
items.ChoiceB: reduced commute time leads to less stress for commuters
items.ChoiceC: downtown workers currently experience significant commute delays
items.ChoiceD: the transit line will be completed on schedule and within budget
items.ChoiceE: productivity is the most important measure of worker performance
items.WhyWrongA: Usage rate is not required for the conclusion — even if only some workers use it, the logic still holds for them.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The argument moves from 'shorter commute' to 'less stressed' — it must assume this connection holds. Negate it: 'shorter commutes do not reduce stress' → the conclusion collapses. Assumption Negation test passes.
items.WhyWrongC: Current delays are not assumed; the argument could hold even if delays are minor.
items.WhyWrongD: Construction logistics are not assumed in moving from shorter commutes to less stress — out of scope for this step.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument does not make productivity a primary metric; this is an irrelevant comparison.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Sales of electric vehicles have increased every year for the past decade, and consumer awareness campaigns about climate change have also grown over the same period. The campaigns must be responsible for the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.Stem: Which of the following is an assumption required by this argument?
items.ChoiceA: The rise in electric vehicle sales is a desirable outcome for policymakers.
items.ChoiceB: Consumer awareness campaigns are sponsored by electric vehicle manufacturers.
items.ChoiceC: Climate change awareness has increased more rapidly than electric vehicle sales.
items.ChoiceD: Electric vehicles are the most effective way to reduce carbon emissions.
items.ChoiceE: No other factors contributed significantly to the rise in electric vehicle sales.
items.WhyWrongA: Policy desirability is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongB: Sponsorship is irrelevant to the causal claim.
items.WhyWrongC: Relative growth rates are not assumed in the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: Effectiveness of EVs for emissions reduction is out of scope; the argument is about the cause of their sales.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. To infer that the campaigns caused the sales increase, the argument must assume that no other explanation (falling battery costs, government subsidies, etc.) is responsible. Negation: 'Other factors did contribute significantly' → the correlation could be coincidental → conclusion fails.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The new tutoring program will improve student test scores, because students who attend tutoring spend more time reviewing material.
items.Stem: The argument depends on assuming which of the following?
items.ChoiceA: The tutoring program is free for all students.
items.ChoiceB: Spending more time reviewing material improves test scores.
items.ChoiceC: Test scores are the best measure of learning.
items.ChoiceD: Students enjoy attending the tutoring program.
items.ChoiceE: Tutoring is the only way to improve test scores.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost is out of scope for the step from 'more review' to 'better scores'.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Negate it — extra review does not improve scores — and the conclusion collapses, so it is required.
items.WhyWrongC: Whether scores are the best measure is an irrelevant comparison.
items.WhyWrongD: Enjoyment is irrelevant to whether scores improve.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument need not claim tutoring is the ONLY route; that is too strong.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The city's new bike-share program will reduce downtown traffic congestion, because many commuters will switch from driving to using shared bikes.
items.Stem: The argument depends on which one of the following assumptions?
items.ChoiceA: Cycling is a healthier form of transportation than driving.
items.ChoiceB: Most downtown commuters live within cycling distance of their workplaces.
items.ChoiceC: At least some commuters who switch to shared bikes would otherwise have driven cars downtown.
items.ChoiceD: The bike-share program will generate enough revenue to sustain itself.
items.ChoiceE: The city will build additional bike lanes to accommodate the new riders.
items.WhyWrongA: Health benefits are out of scope for a claim about congestion.
items.WhyWrongB: 'Most' commuters need not live nearby; the argument needs only that some switch from cars.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. If the switchers would not otherwise have driven (e.g., they used to take the bus), removing them from cars cannot reduce car congestion. Negating this defeats the argument.
items.WhyWrongD: Whether the program pays for itself is irrelevant to whether it eases congestion.
items.WhyWrongE: New bike lanes are not required for the congestion claim to hold.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Installing solar panels on the factory roof will lower the company's monthly energy costs. Therefore, installing the panels is a sound financial decision.
items.Stem: The argument relies on assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: The company currently has no other pressing financial priorities.
items.ChoiceB: The company's energy costs will continue to rise in the years ahead.
items.ChoiceC: Solar power is more environmentally responsible than fossil-fuel power.
items.ChoiceD: Solar panels are the least expensive source of renewable energy available.
items.ChoiceE: The money saved on energy will not be outweighed by the cost of installing and maintaining the panels.
items.WhyWrongA: Other priorities are not required to be absent for this single decision to be sound.
items.WhyWrongB: The savings can make the decision sound even if energy prices merely hold steady.
items.WhyWrongC: Environmental merit does not bear on whether the decision is financially sound.
items.WhyWrongD: The argument compares saving to spending, not solar to other renewables.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. 'Sound financial decision' requires that the savings exceed the costs; if installation and upkeep cost more than is saved, the conclusion collapses.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] This novel will win the national book prize. After all, it has received glowing reviews from every major literary critic.
items.Stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: The book prize is awarded for literary merit rather than for sales.
items.ChoiceB: Receiving glowing reviews from major critics makes a novel likely to win the prize.
items.ChoiceC: This novel is the finest work of fiction published during the year.
items.ChoiceD: No other novel published this year received glowing reviews from the critics.
items.ChoiceE: The critics who praised the novel sit on the prize committee.
items.WhyWrongA: The basis for the award is not something the argument must assume.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The argument leaps from critical acclaim to winning; it must assume acclaim is linked to the prize. Negate it and the reviews give no reason to expect a win.
items.WhyWrongC: Being the best is stronger than being likely to win; it is not required.
items.WhyWrongD: Even if another book was praised, this one could still be likely to win; this is not required.
items.WhyWrongE: The argument need not assume the critics are also judges.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The proposed dam will not harm the river's fish population, because its design includes a fish ladder that allows fish to swim upstream past the dam.
items.Stem: Which one of the following is an assumption required by the argument?
items.ChoiceA: The river's fish population is currently healthy and stable.
items.ChoiceB: The dam will not harm the fish population in any way other than by blocking upstream movement.
items.ChoiceC: Fish are the most important species in the river's ecosystem.
items.ChoiceD: Fish ladders have successfully protected fish populations at other dams.
items.ChoiceE: Building the dam is worth its overall cost to the surrounding environment.
items.WhyWrongA: Current health of the population is not needed for the no-harm conclusion.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. The ladder addresses only upstream movement; if the dam harms fish another way (e.g., changing water temperature), the no-harm conclusion fails. Negation breaks the argument.
items.WhyWrongC: The relative importance of fish is irrelevant to whether they are harmed.
items.WhyWrongD: Success elsewhere would support the claim but is not required for this dam's conclusion.
items.WhyWrongE: Overall worth concerns a different question than whether fish are harmed.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Nutritionist: People who eat breakfast tend to weigh less than people who skip it. So anyone who is trying to lose weight should begin eating breakfast.
items.Stem: The nutritionist's recommendation depends on assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: Eating breakfast improves a person's concentration during the morning.
items.ChoiceB: Most people who are overweight are in the habit of skipping breakfast.
items.ChoiceC: Breakfast is the most important meal of the day.
items.ChoiceD: People who skip breakfast generally do so in an effort to lose weight.
items.ChoiceE: Adding breakfast would not cause those trying to lose weight to consume more total calories over the day.
items.WhyWrongA: Concentration benefits are out of scope for a weight claim.
items.WhyWrongB: The prevalence of skipping among the overweight is not needed for the recommendation.
items.WhyWrongC: The cliché about breakfast's importance is not what the weight-loss advice requires.
items.WhyWrongD: Why skippers skip is irrelevant to whether adding breakfast helps weight loss.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. If adding breakfast simply increases total intake, the dieter would not lose weight; the advice assumes breakfast won't add net calories. Negation defeats it.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Editor: Our magazine should feature more celebrity interviews. Issues that put a celebrity interview on the cover have consistently sold more copies than issues that did not.
items.Stem: The editor's argument assumes which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: Celebrity interviews are inexpensive for the magazine to produce.
items.ChoiceB: Readers prefer celebrity interviews to investigative reporting.
items.ChoiceC: The magazine has always included at least one celebrity interview per issue.
items.ChoiceD: Every issue with a celebrity cover outsold every issue without one.
items.ChoiceE: Increasing the number of celebrity interviews would not cost the magazine more readers than it gains.
items.WhyWrongA: Production cost is not part of the inference from past sales to the recommendation.
items.WhyWrongB: A preference over investigative pieces is not required; the argument rests on sales figures.
items.WhyWrongC: This contradicts the stimulus, which refers to issues without any celebrity interview.
items.WhyWrongD: The claim is about a consistent aggregate pattern, not that every such issue beat every other.
items.WhyWrongE: CORRECT. The move from 'they sold more' to 'feature more' assumes doing so won't backfire; if more interviews drove away other readers, the recommendation would fail.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Since the invention of the printing press, literacy rates in the region rose dramatically. The printing press must therefore be responsible for this rise in literacy.
items.Stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: No factor other than the printing press contributed substantially to the rise in literacy.
items.ChoiceB: Literacy rates in neighboring regions rose at the same time.
items.ChoiceC: The printing press was invented for the express purpose of promoting literacy.
items.ChoiceD: The rise in literacy has been beneficial for the region's economy.
items.ChoiceE: Most books produced on the printing press were read by ordinary people.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. To pin the rise on the press, the argument must rule out other causes (schooling reforms, urbanization); if another factor was responsible, the conclusion fails.
items.WhyWrongB: Neighboring regions are irrelevant to this region's cause.
items.WhyWrongC: The press's purpose does not matter to whether it caused the rise.
items.WhyWrongD: Whether the rise was beneficial is out of scope.
items.WhyWrongE: Who read the books is not required for the causal conclusion.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] The library plans to replace its print journals with online subscriptions to free up shelf space. The change will serve patrons well, since most patrons now prefer to read journals online.
items.Stem: Which one of the following is an assumption on which the argument depends?
items.ChoiceA: Online subscriptions cost the library less than print journals do.
items.ChoiceB: Every patron of the library has reliable access to the internet.
items.ChoiceC: The library's shelves are currently filled to capacity.
items.ChoiceD: The patrons who still prefer print journals will not be significantly disadvantaged by the change.
items.ChoiceE: Reading journals online is more environmentally friendly than printing them.
items.WhyWrongA: Cost savings do not establish that patrons are served well.
items.WhyWrongB: Universal internet access is stronger than needed; the argument needs only that print users aren't badly harmed.
items.WhyWrongC: Whether shelves are full bears on the motive, not on serving patrons well.
items.WhyWrongD: CORRECT. 'Serve patrons well' can't hold if the print-preferring minority is badly hurt; the argument assumes they won't be. Negation undercuts the conclusion.
items.WhyWrongE: Environmental impact is out of scope for serving patrons.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Coach: We lost every game this season in which our star forward did not play. Therefore, if she plays in the championship, we will win it.
items.Stem: The coach's reasoning requires assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: The team's previous losses were due to the star forward's absence rather than to some other factor.
items.ChoiceB: The star forward is the most skilled player on the team.
items.ChoiceC: The team won every game in which the star forward did play.
items.ChoiceD: The team will face a weaker opponent in the championship than in earlier games.
items.ChoiceE: The championship matters more to the team than the earlier games did.
items.WhyWrongA: CORRECT. Only if her absence, not other causes, produced the losses can her presence be expected to yield a win; otherwise the prediction has no basis.
items.WhyWrongB: Being most skilled does not establish that her presence secures a win.
items.WhyWrongC: The stimulus does not claim, and the argument need not assume, that her playing guaranteed past wins.
items.WhyWrongD: A weaker opponent is not something the argument must assume.
items.WhyWrongE: The relative importance of the games is irrelevant to the prediction.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] A new office policy lets employees work from home two days a week. Average productivity has risen since the policy began, so the policy has made employees more productive.
items.Stem: The argument depends on the assumption that
items.ChoiceA: productivity would have fallen sharply had the policy not been adopted.
items.ChoiceB: no factor other than the work-from-home policy accounts for the rise in productivity.
items.ChoiceC: productivity is the most important measure of an employee's value.
items.ChoiceD: employees prefer working from home to working in the office.
items.ChoiceE: every employee took advantage of the option to work from home.
items.WhyWrongA: The argument claims the policy raised productivity, not that productivity would otherwise have collapsed.
items.WhyWrongB: CORRECT. Attributing the rise to the policy requires ruling out other causes (new tools, seasonal demand); if another factor is responsible, the conclusion fails.
items.WhyWrongC: How important productivity is does not bear on whether the policy caused it to rise.
items.WhyWrongD: Employee preference does not establish that the policy caused the productivity gain.
items.WhyWrongE: Universal uptake is stronger than the argument needs.

items.Source: SYNTHETIC — placeholder item, not a real LSAT question
items.Stimulus: [SYNTHETIC] Ecologist: When sea otters vanished from the kelp forest, sea urchins multiplied and devoured the kelp until the forest collapsed. Reintroducing the otters will therefore restore the kelp forest.
items.Stem: The argument depends on assuming which one of the following?
items.ChoiceA: Sea otters are the only animals capable of limiting sea urchin numbers.
items.ChoiceB: The kelp forest was more valuable before the collapse than it is after it.
items.ChoiceC: Nothing that has changed since the collapse will keep the kelp from regrowing once the urchins are reduced.
items.ChoiceD: Sea urchins are able to feed on nothing other than kelp.
items.ChoiceE: Other kelp forests have recovered after their otters were reintroduced.
items.WhyWrongA: The argument needs otters to control urchins, not to be the sole possible controller; negating this leaves it intact.
items.WhyWrongB: Relative value does not bear on whether reintroduction restores the forest.
items.WhyWrongC: CORRECT. Negate it — some change now blocks kelp regrowth — and reintroducing otters would fail to restore the forest.
items.WhyWrongD: What else urchins can eat does not affect whether reducing them lets kelp return.
items.WhyWrongE: Recovery elsewhere is at most supporting evidence, not something the argument must assume.
